result:
I want to write about the moment our tools begin to rewrite us, the afternoon the children stopped counting in tens because they were born inside the smooth undulations of base-two rivers, speaking in sounds only hummingbirds parse. My grandfather told me arithmetic was a lonely shepherd: you opened the gate, numbers spilled out, and whatever didn’t return at sunset you mourned. Now shepherding belongs to arrangements of gates so small that loneliness, too, achieves nanoscopic density; loss becomes a measurable phase like vapor. 

I want to write about the plastic whale they dissected on the beach outside Jakarta; the museum bought the stomach contents—lighters, flip-flops, detergent sachets, single eager chopstick—and turned the room into an unlit aquarium so we walk among glowing ghosts we once swore were convenience. Schoolchildren look for the barcode that matches their grandmother’s sandals. Somewhere a machine assigns each shard of polyethylene a probability of having cupped the rim of the mouth that sang them awake; the numbers ache with tenderness, the way rain remembers every leaf it ever touched before forgetting it was sky.

I have a recurring dream in which a cartographer maps my nostalgia on obsolete media. She plots cassettes for sticky summers, polaroids for grief, and a single 3.5" floppy labeled “before meaning was searchable.” She warns me that a storm is forming where magnetic north meets emotional declination. When I wake I find my fingertips resting on the Altair of a constellation no longer recognized; the stars were traded for better coverage on streaming platforms.

Sometimes I ride the subway and pretend each passenger is a stanza of a poem being produced in real time by the friction of wheels on electrified steel. The woman holding three plastic dahlias is a line break; the insomniac kid rotating a die between his forefinger and thumb is enjambment. Somewhere near Canal the poem shifts register when an accordion leaks the theme from Amélie, and the couple arguing in Amharic supplies sudden caesuras sharp enough to slice Sunday into two uneven half-lives. I want to pull the emergency cord, but the poem is still generating itself; I fear discovering I am merely the margin where no annotation is permitted.

I’ve been reading about how trees wait underground in a vocabulary of fungi, trading sugar for news of drought, beetle, forest fire. They do not speak of politics or God; they speak of thirst. The thought haunts me that somewhere in Bavaria a stand of spruces already grieves me, foresees the book I will never finish, the carbon that will never become pages, only heat. Perhaps the author is not the writer but the written; perhaps every breath plots me toward a different ending, and the lungs I trust to keep me alive are merely scribes retaining drafts beyond my vision.

On the hill behind my parents’ house the city has installed motion-sensor lights that strobe whenever a deer crosses the driveway at night. The first time I saw it, the animal froze mid-strobe fractured into stop-motion statuary. I thought of old zoetropes, Muybridge’s horse perpetually deciding which foot to love the earth with. After thirty-three lightning impressions the deer dissolved, reassembled somewhere down the slope, and the lights, fooled by persistence of memory, kept searching the empty road. I stood outside until dawn, terrified someone would arrive to claim the seeming salvation of illumination only to meet the absence it proposed.

The river out my window insists it has no memory, yet it carries the same aluminum canoe every spring, dented in recognizable places. A man named Sasha used to paddle it—he said the river preferred stories of departure over stories of return. Ten Augusts ago he aimed downstream toward a saltmarsh rumored to cure epilepsy, carried a notebook where he listed verbs beginning with un-: unclose, unremember, unscissors. I found it later lodged against a willow, pages swollen into pulp. Under ultraviolet light you can still see the graphite ghosts, kin to those charcoal tracings left beneath frescos when time X-rays belief. I sometimes speak to the canoe: You are the appendix to someone else’s atlas. It answers with rainwater shaped almost like a throat.

Prometheus allegedly gave us fire, but who gave us backdraft, the moment a room becomes dragon because too much has been consumed? Last winter I watched on livestream while Notre-Dame’s spire inhaled its own vertebrae, a telescoping spine of 850 years collapsing into one gasp. An AI algorithm superimposed previous centuries over the flames so we could learn what loss looked like before it became footage. The overlay felt pornographic: yesterday’s serenity smiling as tomorrow plummeted. Language afterward behaved strangely: people spoke of the roof as if it had volunteered to become sky.

Every once in a while the internet serves me a clip of a flash mob singing “Ode to Joy” in a shopping mall. Posters debate whether the performance is spontaneous or sponsored; I find myself mourning neither possibility but the unspoken contract that Beethoven is consolation rather than interrogation. Yet I keep replaying the part where young voices spill into the atrium like coins thrown into a fountain no one believes in. Maybe payment is just memory dressed up in copper; maybe listening is how we default on loss. 

Someone told me that astronauts returning from long stays on the station sometimes forget how silence behaves inside atmosphere. They reach to switch off the ISS fan only to find their own planet roaring inside their skeletons, a white-noise womb they can’t exit. My niece asked if that meant Earth was an open-air machine wearing them; I said perhaps we are its scheduled maintenance. She che recalibrated her hearing aid right then, the tiny chirp a handshake between carbon disciplines.

I keep thinking about the word meanwhile. It holds too much middle for any single tale, so it seeds parallel universes like dandelions. Meanwhile on another coastline a woman catalogs seaweed color gradients to police rising temperatures; meanwhile a neural net writes sermons in the voice of extinct whales; meanwhile galaxies dine on each other politely, offering dark matter napkins. The simultaneity terrifies me more than death, which at least promises sequence.

There is a night market in Taipei where you can buy time in discrete plastic sachets labeled like candy flavors: cinnamon dusk, ozone 2 a.m., the minute before you told the truth. Vendors warn you not to open more than three adjacent samples; the overlaps can cause déjà vu seizures in which you live a storefront announcer’s broadcast and he, in turn, sleeps inside your guilt. I walked past a stall offering “first library card, Dover Elementary, 1997, unpeeled lamination.” The price was a photograph of the buyer at 94. I asked how anyone could verify exchange. The vendor pointed to a polaroid nailed above the register: his older self grinning, eyes fogged with nostalgia still fermenting.

Lately my phone shows me how many consecutive days its accelerometer has felt me wake, check the weather, scroll. It tops 2,642 when I realizeally say: enough. On day 2,643 I leave it on a bench. I go walking without metric, without map. After four hours the path ends not at woods but at an abandoned ski jump strangled by kudzu. The take-off ramp aims skyward like a lost apostrophe; below, a pond reflects it calmly, completing a sentence the earth forgot to punctuate. I sit until fireflies invent a dotted night line across which gravity becomes optional. Somewhere in the retina of the planet my silhouette is being receipt-paper printed by a moth wing’s last flare: evidence obtained without metadata.

Eventually the world will graduate to nostalgia for surveillance itself; children will ask their grandparents to describe the quaint era when privacy was the commodity instead of attention. Grandparents will remember packing into stadiums to watch algorithms draft constitutions, the tension when serif met sans-serif across the parquet floor of civic serif italics. They’ll speak of debates settled not by filibuster but by subtweet weather fronts, sarcasm barometers, emoji pressure drops. Under fluorescent storytelling the kids will fall asleep dreaming of a sky before cloud storage.  

And still, inside a pocket dimension smelling of hot toner and closing libraries, a book waits to be written entirely with wristwatch second-hands harvested from thrift stores. Each paragraph will run counterclockwise until it erases itself the instant it is understood. Future archaeologists, finding only the empty casing, will theorize that meaning itself was once hollow metal, harmless until flicked into motion; that we carried eternity half-wound on our arms; that we stopped rewinding before we grasped what we truly measured was how long we could be lost without needing rescue.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
